options.text: `proxy_pass /api/users;`
`proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/;`

questions.explanation: The article explains the trailing slash behavior. With `location /api/` and `proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000/;`, Nginx replaces the matched prefix and forwards `/users`.

options.text: Remove the trailing slash so Nginx forwards the full original URI: `proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000;`
Keep the trailing slash because every backend should receive stripped paths
Replace `proxy_pass` with `root /api/` so the app router handles the path
Add `try_files $uri /index.html` inside `/api/` so API responses become static files

questions.explanation: If the backend expects `/api/users`, the no-slash form preserves the full original URI. The trailing-slash form is only correct when the backend expects the prefix to be stripped.

options.text: The app is missing original host and scheme context, so it builds URLs from the upstream connection instead of the client-facing request
The app needs a trailing slash on `proxy_pass` because headers cannot affect redirects
The DNS `A` record is wrong because redirect URLs are resolved before HTTP headers
The Nginx worker cannot read static files because `Host` controls filesystem ownership

questions.explanation: The article states that forwarding `Host` and `X-Forwarded-Proto` lets the application know the original domain and whether the client used HTTP or HTTPS. Without them, virtual hosting and URL generation can break.

options.text: `Host` preserves the original domain name for the application
`X-Real-IP` carries the direct client IP as seen by Nginx
`X-Forwarded-For` carries the proxy chain so logs and rate limits can identify clients
`X-Forwarded-Proto` tells the app whether the original request used HTTP or HTTPS
`X-Forwarded-Proto` changes the backend TCP port automatically

questions.explanation: The article describes each of these forwarding headers and why production proxies need them. They pass request context to the app; they do not automatically change which TCP port the backend listens on.

questions.scenario: A rate limiter in the application suddenly treats all users as one client after moving behind Nginx. The app logs show every request from `127.0.0.1`. What is the most likely proxy configuration gap?

options.text: Missing client IP forwarding headers such as `X-Real-IP` and `X-Forwarded-For`
Using `try_files` without a SPA fallback
Setting `index index.html` in the static server block
Lowering DNS TTL before a migration

questions.explanation: Without forwarding headers, the app sees Nginx itself as the client. The article calls out rate limiting and logging as reasons `X-Real-IP` and `X-Forwarded-For` are required.

questions.scenario:
A backend has routes `/users` and `/orders`. Nginx exposes them under `/api/`. The failed access trace shows:

```text
client request: GET /api/users
upstream request: GET /api/users
backend route table: GET /users, GET /orders
backend response: 404
```

Why is this a proxy path bug rather than a missing backend route?

options.text: The backend route exists as `/users`, but Nginx forwarded `/api/users` instead of stripping the external prefix
The backend route table proves `/api/users` exists and should have returned 200
A 404 from any upstream always means the process is offline
Nginx cannot forward paths that begin with `/api/`

questions.explanation: The manifest and trace together show a prefix mismatch. The backend knows `/users`, but it received `/api/users`, which points to the no-slash `proxy_pass` behavior described in the article.

questions.scenario: A developer removes all `proxy_set_header` lines because the app still returns 200 in smoke tests. Which production issue is most likely to appear later?

options.text: Logs, rate limits, virtual hosting, or redirects can use Nginx's local context instead of the real client request context
Nginx will stop parsing the config because `proxy_set_header` is required syntax after every `proxy_pass`
Static assets will always return 404 because headers control `root` lookup
DNS resolution will fail because the resolver reads `X-Forwarded-For`

questions.explanation: A simple 200 check may miss context-dependent behavior. The article emphasizes that forwarding headers are not optional in production because apps need the original host, client IP, proxy chain, and scheme.
